text: raised its foot and kicked the teacher’s rear end . Ronin couldn’t hold back his own smile as the children laughed uncontrollably. A few of them doubled over, holding their stomachs, and one even fell to the floor . The teacher rocked slightly as the other bot kicked it again. Slowly, the teacher spun its head to face behind . “You are disrupting the education of my students .” “I am only following instructions,” the other bot replied . “Desist your behavior or I will issue you a detention slip .” The bot tilted its head to the side and lowered its foot. A second later, it raised its other foot and kicked the teacher again . The teacher — managing a glare, despite its fixed facial features — turned its body with a few lumbering steps. “You have ignored your verbal warning. Printing detention slip .” There was a whirring noise from inside the teacher, but it stopped when a pair of soldiers approached. The students, red-faced, struggled to regain their composure . One of the soldiers wiped the chalk letters off the bot’s back with his sleeve and shot the kids a half-heartedly disapproving look. The other stepped between the two bots, grinning . “Come on, Terence. Mr. Mathers has class to get back to. You don’t have to follow every sign the kids stick on his back .” “You are correct. But it is more fun to do so.” Terence leaned to the side, glancing at the children, and the shutter on one of his optics slipped closed before he walked away. The kids giggled again as Mr. Mathers and the two soldiers turned to face them . “Detention slips for everyone. Printing—ERROR! SPOOL OUT OF PAPER! BLACK INK LOW! CANCELLING PRINT .” “How many times have we told you kids to stop playing pranks on Mr. Mathers ?” The children, as one, glanced down at their shoes . “Listen to him, because he’s got more knowledge than you can begin to understand. He’ll teach you everything you need to know, just like he taught us, and your moms and dads and grandparents .” “Now what do you say to Mr. Mathers?” the other soldier asked . “We’re sorry,” the children said together . “Due to lack of ink and paper, your apology is accepted. We must return immediately to the classroom to resume instruction.” Mr. Mathers turned and walked away . As the children shuffled behind him, one of the soldiers crouched beside a student . “Hey, here’s what you should write the next time …” The soldier’s hushed words were lost in the din of activity as the group moved away . Ronin couldn’t recall witnessing anything like it during his travels. There were places where humans and bots coexisted peacefully — most anywhere outside of Cheyenne, really — but nowhere else where their lives were so integrated. This was what Newton meant. This was how humans and bots were designed to interact . He pushed off the wall and walked toward his quarters; it was time to wake Lara. In the eight days since their first heated exchange, Rodriguez hadn’t attempted to question her again. The Colonel was done waiting . At least she’d forgiven Ronin for the laudanum, even if that forgiveness came with a promise to remove a few of his parts if he did it again . The base’s leaders had been generous enough — at Nancy’s insistence, most likely — to provide he and Lara with a room outside the infirmary. It was sparsely furnished, with only a double bed, a small table, and a footlocker, but it had a working door and was warm and dry. More than they’d hoped for, upon leaving Cheyenne . Lara was already awake when he entered their room. She smiled up at him, and he couldn’t help noticing the light in her eyes. The swelling was nearly gone, and most of her bruises had faded to yellow . “Feel better after your nap?” he asked . Her smile faded. “I couldn’t sleep. Not looking forward to being inter…intro…what was the word ?” “ Interrogated .” “Yeah. That. I don’t know what the hell he thinks he’s going to get out of me.” Lara rose slowly, her movements stiff. She could walk around without help now, but the subtle changes to her expression often belied her discomfort. She adjusted the lay of her sling around her neck. “And just because I’m cooled down doesn’t mean I’m not still pissed, or that I won’t blow up on them again .” “Make an effort, at least, to think about things from their side .” “I have, and I get it. I would’ve done a lot to protect Tabitha if I had the chance. But even at my lowest, when I didn’t know where my next meal was coming from, I still helped people out. Because that’s how things are supposed to be. We’re not…we’re not supposed to see other people suffering and just look away, pretending nothing happened .” “They inherited this situation, Lara. They were born into it .” “And I was born to die under Warlord’s boot?” she countered. “All those people there, they only exist to be his entertainment? You’ve got all those bots who follow his lead because it’s too dangerous not to, all those humans living in terror…and the whole time, two miles away, there were enough soldiers with enough firepower to end him, and all these people did was hide. They might as well have been the ones who left us there to die .” “They didn’t leave you, Lara,” Ronin said. His processors hung up on the thought of her being left to die, nearly calling up images he didn’t want to see again . “You crossed Warlord,” she said, stepping close. “You risked yourself to protect me. Why? Why did you go out of your way to bury Tabitha? You’re supposed to operate on logic or some shit,
